title: End-to-End Release, Incident Recovery, and Engineering Defense · Go Build Room
summary: A regional field-research team incrementally builds defensible recoverable Go Pokedex release dossier from original fixed PokéAPI fixtures while retaining prior Go, HTTP, Git, quality-gate, accessibility, security, testing, cancellation, and recovery requirements. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of defensible recoverable Go Pokedex release dossier while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal…
objectives: Reconcile source, module graph, fixtures, tests, binary, command behavior, saved data, checksum, documentation, and stakeholder acceptance to one revision.
Rehearse invalid input, API not found, timeout, malformed response, cancellation, cache corruption, write interruption, disk failure, and signal shutdown.
Prove forward migration, old-binary compatibility limits, backup, rollback, restored collection integrity, and support decision points.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Pokedex product engineering and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Pokedex Go E2e Reconcilia…
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop Go team handles case 9117 during a clean cross-platform package smoke. Build deterministic Go evidence to defend reconciled release, migration, rollback, restore, recovery, and residual-risk ownership; no user-visible meaning may rely on color or cursor motion; then change one catch, owned record, encounter relation, or history state and prove the prior invariant. Browser Go uses pure bounded models and original fixtures only. Real PokéAPI traffic, DNS, sockets, TLS, host files, terminals, signals, race and leak claims, load, cross-platform packaging, accessibility review, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Reconcile source, module graph, fixtures, tests, binary, command behavior, saved data, checksum, documentation, and stakeholder acceptance to one revision.
